source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.1 IP addressing (pp. 220-222)

questions.q: In a subnet mask, which bits mark the network part and which the host part of an address?
options: The consecutive 1s mark the network bits; the consecutive 0s mark the host bits
The 0s are the network bits; the 1s are the host bits
The first byte is always the network; the rest is host
There is no relationship between mask bits and network/host
questions.why: The subnet mask's leading 1s define the network bits and its trailing 0s define the host bits.

questions.q: What does the CIDR notation 192.168.1.10/24 mean?
options: The first 24 bits are the network part (mask 255.255.255.0)
There are 24 usable hosts
The address is on VLAN 24
The 24th host on the network
questions.why: /24 means 24 network bits, i.e. the mask 255.255.255.0.

questions.q: How is the network address (NetID) of a host obtained?
options: By a bitwise logical AND between the IP address and the subnet mask
By adding the IP and the mask
By a logical OR between the IP and the mask
By reversing the IP address

questions.q: How do you get the broadcast address of a network?
options: Keep the network bits unchanged and set all host bits to 1
Set all bits to 1
Keep the host bits and zero the network bits
Add 1 to the network address
questions.why: The broadcast address keeps the network bits and turns every host bit to 1 (e.g. 192.168.1.255 for 192.168.1.10/24).

questions.q: Which two addresses in a range have special roles and cannot be assigned to a host?
options: The first (network address) and the last (broadcast address)
The first two addresses
The last two addresses
Only the broadcast address
questions.why: The first address is the network address and the last is the broadcast address; neither can be given to a terminal machine.

options: Its 1s are consecutive from left to right (e.g. 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000)
It contains an equal number of 1s and 0s
It starts with a 0
Any pattern of bits is valid
questions.why: A legitimate mask has its 1 bits contiguous from the left; a pattern like 11001001... is illegitimate.
